Ntekq2MAAAAJ.keywords: Human Computer Interaction, Inclusive Design, Assistive Technology, Workplace Accommodations, Universal Design, Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC), Sonification, Informal Learning Environments, Dementia Communication, Web Accessibility
Ntekq2MAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Carrie Bruce is a Principal Research Scientist at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where she leads and contributes to interdisciplinary accessibility and rehabilitation research within Georgia Tech’s human-centered computing and inclusive design efforts, including work associated with the *Accessible Aquarium Project* and collaborations with speech‑language pathology and rehabilitation engineering teams. Her work centers on the design and evaluation of technologies and environments that support participation for people with disabilities, with particular emphasis on <u>assistive technology</u>, <u>inclusive design</u>, and <u>workplace accommodations</u>. She combines empirical assessment, design interventions, and applied deployment to improve accessibility across *informal learning environments*, *communication contexts*, and *built and digital environments*.

---

## Research Areas
Bruce’s research spans Human–Computer Interaction (HCI), rehabilitation and assistive technologies, and universal/inclusive design for both physical and digital spaces. She has contributed to the development and application of the International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health (ICF) for assessment ("Rehabilitation and health assessment: applying ICF guidelines") and to frameworks that treat the physical environment as an independent factor in performance ("The physical environment as an independent measure"). A sustained strand of her work explores non‑visual, sound‑based exhibit interpretation and interactive sonification for accessibility in informal learning environments (e.g., "Aquarium sonification: Soundscapes for accessible dynamic informal learning environments", "Designing interactive sonification for live aquarium exhibits", "Aquarium fugue"), producing design guidance and evaluation methods for dynamic, real‑time exhibit mediation. Another prominent line addresses communication and participation at work and in care settings: corpus development and discourse analysis for Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC) users ("The AAC and non‑AAC workplace corpus (ANAWC)", "Linguistic characteristics of AAC discourse in the workplace"), and development/evaluation of environmental and communication assessment tools for dementia care ("Development and evaluation of the environment and communication assessment toolkit with speech‑language pathologists", "Evaluating and modifying the communication environment for people with dementia"). She also engages with broader topics in workplace accommodation, universal design practice ("The state of the science in universal design"), and practical accessibility tooling and professional practice (recent work on web accessibility tools and UX professionals’ experiences in accessibility).

## Notable Works
- "Rehabilitation and health assessment: applying ICF guidelines" (2009) — A highly cited contribution that applies the ICF framework to rehabilitation assessment practice, informing how functional abilities and environmental factors are measured and integrated into intervention planning.  
- "Aquarium sonification: Soundscapes for accessible dynamic informal learning environments" (2006) and follow‑on interactive sonification work (2012–2013) — A program of research that designs, implements, and evaluates sound‑based interpretive systems to make dynamic aquarium exhibits accessible to visitors with vision impairments, advancing methods for real‑time, multisensory exhibit mediation.  
- "The AAC and non‑AAC workplace corpus (ANAWC)" and "Linguistic characteristics of AAC discourse in the workplace" (2013, 2019 overview) — Creation and analysis of a novel corpus to characterize workplace discourse of AAC users versus non‑AAC peers, yielding insights that inform AAC vocabulary design, workplace accommodations, and communication assessment practices.

## Academic Background
Carrie Bruce’s publication record reflects longstanding, interdisciplinary collaborations across rehabilitation science, speech‑language pathology, HCI, and museum/informal learning research. As a Principal Research Scientist at Georgia Tech she has contributed to federally supported and center‑based efforts in universal design and rehabilitation engineering, collaborated with speech‑language pathologists and occupational/rehabilitation professionals, and led applied projects translating assessment frameworks into practice (e.g., toolkits for dementia care and environmental assessment). Her work has been recognized through sustained citation impact (total citations ≈ 499), multiple funded project contributions (including RERC and SBIR related activities), and leadership in projects that bridge research, design, and practitioner communities to improve access, participation, and workplace inclusion for people with disabilities.
